title: Lesson 3: Present Imperfective Tense & Word Structure   اوسني نا-کامل زمان او د کلمې جوړښت
description: Unit 3 marks your first encounter with core Pashto verbal morphology: the Present Imperfective Tense. You will learn to express habitual actions, discuss family and daily routines, and navigate early word structure rules.
duration: Textbook pp. 40–51 (approx. 10-15 pages of primary instruction plus Workbook exercises)

content.heading: Module 1: The Present Imperfective Tense
lines: Welcome to Unit 3! It is time to learn how to talk about ongoing, habitual, or daily routines.
To do this, we use the Present Imperfective Tense. In Pashto, verb agreement is based on the person (1st, 2nd, or 3rd) and number (singular or plural) of the subject.

Let's look at some essential activity verbs. Notice that from this unit onward, romanised transliteration is only provided at the point of introduction: [xoṛ-] (eat), [ost-] (live/reside), [waḍ-/wač-] (read), [līk-] (write), [kāṛ-] (work), [zda kaw-] (study/learn), [gor-] (look/see).

content.text: Dialect & Pronunciation Pro-Tip: Be aware of a major regional difference! In the 3rd person singular present tense, Kabuli speakers use the ending [da], whereas Kandahari speakers often use [ka]. Also, remember that in the present imperfective, the stress generally falls on the verb's final syllable.

content.heading: Verb Paradigms & SOV Examples
lines: 1st Person: I live = zə osəm (زه اوسم) | We live = mung osu (موږ اوسو)
2nd Person: You (sg) live = tə ose (ته اوسې) | You (pl) live = tāso osay (تاسو اوسئ)
3rd Person: He/She lives = hagha osi (هغه اوسي) | They live = hagho osi (هغوی اوسي)
Now, let's look at them in strict SOV sentences:
احمد په کابل کې اوسي. [Ahmad pa kābul ke osi.] (Ahmad lives in Kabul.)
ما کتاب لولم / ما کتاب وډم. [Mā kitāb waḍam.] (I read a book — note the future imperfective nuance here.)

content.heading: Module 2: Family, Home Life & Irregular Nouns
lines: Let's expand your vocabulary with family terms: [plār] (father), [mor] (mother), [wror] (brother), [khōr] (sister), [kor] (house/family).
Pashto has a complex word structure that includes gender classes and case inflection. Some nouns are irregular.

For example, you will encounter masculine nouns that take feminine oblique forms when used with prepositions. The word 'brother' is [wror] in its standard form, but becomes [wrōr] in the oblique case!

content.text: Cultural Context: The Afghan household is traditionally a multigenerational extended family. The eldest son plays a critical role in family structure and responsibilities. Sociolinguistic Note: When writing or reading dialogues set in traditional or rural areas, avoid scenarios depicting women in public unaccompanied, as this contradicts traditional norms.

content.question: Which of the following best describes the function of the Present Imperfective Tense in Pashto?
options: It describes actions that were completed entirely in the past.
It describes habitual, ongoing actions or daily routines.
It is used exclusively to issue commands or instructions.
It alters the sentence structure from SOV to SVO.
content.explanation: The Present Imperfective Tense is used to signify unbounded, habitual, or ongoing actions (like 'Ahmad lives in Kabul' or 'I read a book daily').

content.heading: Module 3: Extended Reading & Pashto Folk Literature
lines: This unit features your first extended reading completely in the Pashto script. Remember, transliteration is now scaffolding, not the primary text.
To enrich your cultural understanding, every unit includes a 'Diversion'—a Pashto proverb or folk-literature item.

content.heading: Proverb of the Unit (متل)
lines: د غره په سر هم لار شته
Translation: Even over the mountain, there is a path.

Cultural Commentary: This famous Pashto proverb reflects the resilience embedded in Pashtun culture. The mountainous geography of Afghanistan makes travel incredibly difficult, yet this proverb serves as a metaphor for hope and determination: no matter how insurmountable an obstacle or problem appears, there is always a way forward or a solution to be found.

content.question: In the Pashto present imperfective tense, where does the phonological stress typically fall on the verb?
options: On the negative particle before the verb.
Verbs are entirely unstressed in Pashto.
content.explanation: In the present imperfective forms, the phonological stress typically falls on the verb-final syllable.
